svg.text: BIBLICAL HEBREW
SCRIPT — ABJAD WRITING SYSTEMS
HEBREW SQUARE SCRIPT (ASSYRIAN BLOCK)
22 consonant letters; right-to-left; no lowercase
Square script adopted from Aramaic during Babylonian exile (~586 BCE)
Earlier: Paleo-Hebrew (Phoenician-type); used on coins until 135 CE
NIKUD: VOWEL POINTING SYSTEM
Masoretes (Tiberias, ~600-900 CE) invented vowel points (nikud)
Points written above, below, or inside consonants
qamatz = /a/ • patah = /a/ • tsere = /e/ • holam = /o/
Biblical text: always pointed • Modern Israeli text: usually unpointed
FINAL LETTER FORMS
5 letters change form at word end: kaf mem nun pe tsadi
Final kaf ך • final mem ם • final nun ן • final pe ף • final tsadi ץ
SAMPLE BIBLICAL TEXT
Bereshit bara Elohim et ha-shamayim ve-et ha-aretz.
"In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth." (Gen 1:1)
ARABIC · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ARABIC ABJAD
28 consonant letters; right-to-left (same direction as Hebrew)
Arabic has 6 more consonants than Hebrew (emphatics + uvulars)
Arabic letters change shape: initial / medial / final / isolated
Hebrew letters do NOT connect (except 5 final forms)
ARABIC HARAKAT (VOWEL MARKS)
Arabic also adds vowels as diacritics above/below consonants
fatha = /a/ • kasra = /i/ • damma = /u/ • sukun = no vowel
Used in Quran and learning texts; adult texts normally unvoweled
ABJAD COMPARISON
Both: consonantal abjad, right-to-left, vowels optional
Both: vowel pointing systems added by scholars for sacred texts
Hebrew: 22 letters, block shapes • Arabic: 28, cursive connected
Both descended from Phoenician script via different paths
PLATE 09a ALT · BIBLICAL HEBREW / ARABIC · SCRIPT
